items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения... У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation: I’m tormented again by vague doubts... Shpak has a tape recorder, the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.justification: The opening phrase preserves the mock-heroic register well, but the clause У Шпака — ... / у посла — ... in the Russian conveys that each has lost the respective item (the items are no longer with them). The actual English renders it as present possession ('Shpak has... the ambassador has...'), which flips the meaning and removes the callback's comic implication that the items are missing. This is a substantive mistranslation that alters the joke.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, Your Majesty, very nice to meet you. Your Majesty, very nice to meet you, Your Majesty. Very nice to meet you, hello, Your Majesty.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.justification: Reference analysis stresses that Bunsha is absurdly introducing himself as 'the Tsar' (not addressing an existing tsar). The actual translation turns every line into an address ('Your Majesty'), making Bunsha seem deferential to a sovereign rather than drunkenly claiming the title. That change removes a key comic clash (a petty Soviet bureaucrat grandiosely introducing himself as the Tsar) and thus undermines the intended effect.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.justification: Matches the reference in content and register: the abrupt, pompous demand is preserved and appropriately comedic in the context.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали.
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.justification: Literal and faithful to the reference; preserves the archaic, regal tone that creates the contrast with the modern setting.

items.translation: Bricked in! Bricked in, you demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.justification: The reference analysis specifies that 'demons' are the logical subject causing the entrapment (not being directly addressed). The actual translation turns 'demons' into a vocative ('you demons!'), changing the intended grammar and nuance: instead of lamenting that demons have walled him in, Ivan is addressing/insulting them. This shifts the dramatic/comic effect and misrepresents the original construction.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.justification: Equivalent to the reference; preserves the archaic religious register and the comedic juxtaposition with modern technology.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.justification: Both renditions invite mass participation in dancing, but the reference's idiom ('The floor is open to everyone!') carries a slightly more theatrical/announcer flavor that ties into the scene's party/dance milieu. 'Everyone dance!' is a perfectly serviceable imperative and retains the meaning and energy but flattens the idiomatic stage/announcer register a bit. This is a small stylistic loss rather than a meaning failure.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.justification: The actual translation conveys the speaker's pleased surprise at entering an apartment full of scarce goods. It does not imply the speaker is leaving (a concern in the reference analysis). The idiom differs but preserves the comic implication of fortunate timing; no substantive loss.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Imported caviar... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.justification: The reference warns explicitly not to call the eggplant spread 'caviar' (that inversion is the joke: expensive caviar vs humble 'eggplant caviar' as a Soviet canned spread). The actual translation labels it 'Imported caviar... eggplant!' which risks reading as if the eggplant itself is a caviar rather than an inexpensive 'spread' being presented as if it were a delicacy. This misrenders the ironic contrast and can confuse the gag.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.justification: Translation follows the reference closely, preserving the escalating list and the comedic hiccup in counting (the trailing 'Three'). The register and joke are kept intact.

items.original: И тебя вылечат!  И тебя тоже вылечат!  И меня вылечат!
items.translation: And they’ll cure you!  And you’ll be cured too!  And I’ll be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.justification: Meaning, tone and melodic repetition are preserved. The translation keeps the resigned, repetitive cadence that turns a dark subject into a comic refrain.

summary.interpretation: 2.82 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.56 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
